title: [Chat] Retry on 'ask' chat drops chapter+characters+worldNotes context (LLM acts blind)
description:
On a Chat (kind='ask') retry, the LLM receives no chapter content, no character context, and no world notes — it responds as if it only has the bare conversation history. Reproduces by clicking Regenerate on any Chat assistant message; the new reply will not match the chapter's voice/POV and may explicitly say "I don't see any previous parts of the story."

## Root cause

For action='ask', `backend/src/services/prompt.service.ts:255-264` puts worldNotes + characters + chapterBlock + taskBlock into the synthesised USER message (not the system message). The system message contains only the generic system prompt.

`backend/src/routes/chat.routes.ts:476-478` sends to Venice:
- normal turn: `[systemMsg, ...history, synthesisedUserMsg]` — context present
- retry turn: `[systemMsg, ...history]` — synthesisedUserMsg dropped

History is built from `priorMessagesForHistory.map(...)` at lines 447-472. Each user turn becomes `{role: 'user', content: m.contentJson}` (bare text). The 'ask' branch with attachment reframes as `User question: ...` but still doesn't include chapter/characters/worldNotes.

Result: on retry of an 'ask' chat, Venice receives no chapter context.

Scene chats are unaffected because action='scene' puts all context into the SYSTEM message (`prompt.service.ts:236-247`), which is preserved on retry.

## Why no test caught this

`backend/tests/routes/chat.test.ts` retry tests assert:
- `body.retry === true` reaches the wire (case C, case B)
- assistant count post-retry (1, not 2 — linear-replace)
- new content matches what Venice replied

None inspect the messages array sent to Venice for chapter/world/character content. An assertion like "body.messages.some(m => m.content.includes('Chapter so far'))" would fail today.

## Provenance

The synthesisedUserMsg-drop-on-retry was introduced by [SC6] (Scene tab) before PR #90 (AI Surfaces Unification). The [SC6] comment at `chat.routes.ts:473-475` reads "On retry the trailing user turn is already in history; do NOT append synthesisedUserMsg again or the model would see a duplicate user turn." That reasoning is correct for the BARE user text but misses that synthesisedUserMsg carries chapter context that the bare history version doesn't.

PR #90's Task 2 (deleteAllAfter wiring) re-touched this region but didn't change the message-array assembly; the bug pre-existed.

## Fix paths

(a) Filter `lastUserMsg.id` out of priorMessagesForHistory on retry, then use the unified `[systemMsg, ...history, synthesisedUserMsg]` for both branches. Smallest, cleanest change.

(b) Replace the lastUserMsg entry in history with the synthesised content (rebuild that entry via the same renderer the prompt uses).

(c) Move chapter/characters/worldNotes blocks into the SYSTEM message for action='ask' to match scene's structure. Larger blast radius (touches every ask call, not just retry) but eliminates this asymmetry permanently.

(a) is the smallest change and matches the existing logic shape. (c) is the architecturally cleaner long-term fix.

## Acceptance

- A retry of a Chat 'ask' message produces a Venice request whose user message contains "Chapter so far:" (when the chapter has content) and the same world-notes / characters blocks as the original turn.
- Add a backend test asserting the messages array on retry includes chapter context (specifically: an entry whose content includes "Chapter so far:").
- Existing case C / case B / linear-replace semantics remain correct.
- Scene retry remains unaffected.
notes:
verify: cd backend && npm test -- chat.test.ts

NOTE: do NOT take the stopgap fix path (a) — see story-editor-k1r for the architectural fix that closes this as a side effect. This issue stays open as the user-visible symptom; closes when k1r lands.

title: [Frontend] Extract useStreamingAI primitive; consolidate Chat/Scene/AI-Completion streaming orchestration
description:
Three AI-streaming hooks each re-implement the same AbortController + apiStream + parseAiSseStream event-loop + error-mapping dance:

- Scene: streamMessage() in frontend/src/lib/api.ts:428 + useSceneTranscript.ts (imperative callbacks)
- Chat: useSendChatMessageMutation in frontend/src/hooks/useChat.ts:222 (TanStack mutation; abort wiring landed in bw2)
- AI Completion: useAICompletion.ts (bespoke hook with its own callback shape; used by Continue Writing + inline AI selection bubble)

The shared primitives (apiStream, parseAiSseStream) handle the wire format. What's duplicated three times is the orchestration above them: ~30 lines of AbortController + 'for await' event loop + done/chunk/error mapping + abort-signal-checked early returns. ~90 lines of structural copies.

The split is downstream-state-shape-driven, not protocol-driven:
- Scene has candidate cards (each assistant turn is an insertable card)
- Chat has the optimistic-bubble + cache-invalidation pattern (TanStack)
- AI Completion has inline TipTap editor decorations

The streaming machinery itself is identical across all three.

Maintenance debt is already visible: streamMessage's JSDoc has a 'TODO: expose an onCitations callback when a scene-tab caller needs it' comment. When the next AI feature lands, the implementer picks a flavour and copies a third implementation. This will only get worse as more AI features are added.

Goal: extract a useStreamingAI primitive (or stream-shaped utility — design decision) that owns AbortController + apiStream + parseAiSseStream loop + error mapping. Each consumer wraps it with its feature-specific state plumbing. Net: ~100-150 lines of deletion, one mental model for AI streaming in this app.

Acceptance:
- One shared streaming primitive with the abort + event-loop dance.
- Scene's streamMessage(), useSendChatMessageMutation, and useAICompletion all migrate to use it.
- All three downstream state shapes (candidate buffer, chat draft store, editor decorations) preserved unchanged.
- Existing tests for all three paths pass.
- streamMessage's onCitations TODO is resolved by the consolidation (in-flight citations available to all consumers if they want them).

Out of scope: changing any of the three downstream state shapes; backend changes; the SSE protocol itself.
design:
## Why three implementations exist (design history)

Each path was written when the protocol changed, and the implementer copied whichever sibling existed at the time (imperative vs TanStack vs custom hook). Nobody sat down to design the abstraction. The split is a fossil of incremental development, not an architectural decision.

## Per-event behaviour comparison

What each consumer does with each SSE event type:

| Step                | Scene                                          | Chat                                              | AI Completion                                       |
|---------------------|------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------|
| Endpoint            | /chats/:id/messages                            | /chats/:id/messages                               | /ai/complete                                        |
| Body                | {content, retry, modelId}                      | adds attachment, enableWebSearch                  | totally different shape                             |
| Open + abort + loop | identical                                      | identical                                         | identical                                           |
| chunk event         | extract delta.content -> push to buffer        | extract delta.content -> push to bubble           | extract delta.content -> append to text             |
| done event          | finishAssistant(localId)                       | markDone() + invalidate query                     | flip status to 'done'                               |
| error event         | failAssistant(msg)                             | markError({code,msg}) + throw                     | flip status to 'error' + push to error store        |
| citations event     | ignored                                        | sent through to message cache                     | ignored                                             |
| Abort handling      | catch AbortError -> reset stream               | (gap — bw2 fixes)                                 | check signal.aborted -> early-return                |

Rows 3+ all differ only in WHICH callback the consumer plugs in. The streaming machinery itself is identical.

## Things that look different but aren't

- Different endpoints: that's a parameter, not divergence.
- Different body shapes: opaque to the streamer; pass through.
- Different state sinks: those are callbacks the consumer provides.
- Citations sometimes wanted: optional onCitations callback, default ignore.
- AI Completion harvests rate-limit headers: optional onResponseHeaders callback, or expose Response once.

## Things that ARE per-consumer (stay in the wrapper, not the primitive)

- Scene's pushUser -> beginStreamingAssistant -> finishAssistant transitions (candidate-card lifecycle).
- Chat's optimistic-bubble seed + cache invalidation on done.
- AI Completion's thinking -> streaming -> done state machine + tinted TipTap decoration.
- Re-entrant policies: AI Completion aborts in-flight before starting new (single-slot completion); Chat queues serially via TanStack mutation; Scene appends.

These are downstream concerns, not stream concerns. They belong in each wrapping hook, not in the streaming primitive.

## Suggested primitive shape (for the design pass)

```ts
function useStreamingAI<TBody>(opts: {
  endpoint: string;
  body: TBody;
  onChunk: (delta: string) => void;
  onCitations?: (citations: ...) => void;       // optional
  onResponseHeaders?: (res: Response) => void;  // optional, for rate-limit harvesting
  onDone: () => void;
  onError: (err: ApiError) => void;
}): { start: () => Promise<void>; stop: () => void; isStreaming: boolean }
```

Each consumer wraps it:
- Scene: hook callbacks drive useSceneTranscriptStore actions.
- Chat: TanStack mutation wraps start(); onChunk/onDone/onError drive useChatDraftStore.
- AI Completion: hook wraps start() with thinking->streaming->done state machine on local component state.

## Honest cost-benefit

Benefit of current split: zero. Each path duplicates the same dance.

Cost of consolidation: medium effort. All three call sites have integration tests; consolidating means re-running all three end-to-end flows. The chat draft store and the scene candidate model are non-trivial. Estimate: 1-2 days of focused work + review.

Cost of NOT consolidating: it gets worse as features land. The streamMessage onCitations TODO is the canary — Scene will eventually need in-flight citations and the choice will be (a) grow streamMessage to feature-parity with the mutation, or (b) move Scene to TanStack. Either way the duplication keeps pulling work.
notes:
plan: docs/superpowers/plans/2026-05-09-ai-surfaces-unification.md
verify: cd frontend && npm run typecheck && npm test && npm run lint:design

title: bd-close-reviewed: handle already-closed bd issue gracefully
description:
scripts/bd-close-reviewed.sh --phase=close calls `bd close <id>` without checking whether the issue is already closed. If a controller invokes the skill twice (interrupted session, manual re-run, network blip), `bd close` may error or no-op depending on bd version.

Make it explicitly idempotent: check `bd show <id> --json` for status=closed first; exit 0 silently if already closed (with a one-line stdout note). The override-recording path (--override-block) should also short-circuit on already-closed (don't append another override line, don't create another empty trailer commit).

Verify is weak as filed (needs a sacrificial closed bd issue to exercise the dual-close path); tighten when implementing.
notes:
plan: trivial — small script change; defensive only
verify: bash scripts/test/bd-close-reviewed-idempotent.test.sh

title: [Backend] Unify prompt-building: one canonical message-array shape across all actions
description:
The retry-loses-chapter-context bug (story-editor-9ph) surfaced a deeper asymmetry: `buildPrompt` in `backend/src/services/prompt.service.ts` produces structurally different message arrays per action, and that asymmetry leaks into other parts of the system that assume a uniform shape (most visibly: the chat retry path drops the synthesised user message, which silently drops chapter context for `ask` but not `scene`).

Goal: find one common method for building the different prompts across all parts of the app, with the message-array shape uniform across actions. Don't ship a stopgap on 9ph that perpetuates the divergence — fix the underlying construction instead.

## Current asymmetry

`prompt.service.ts:174-269` — single `buildPrompt(input)` function with two structurally different return paths:

**`scene` action** (lines 232-251): all context blocks live in the system message; user message is the bare freeformInstruction.

```
system:  systemContent + worldNotesBlock + charactersBlock + chapterBlock + sceneTemplate
user:    freeformInstruction
```

**All other actions** — `ask`, `continue`, `rephrase`/`rewrite`, `expand`, `summarise`, `describe`, `freeform` (lines 253-269): context blocks live in the user message; system message is just the generic prompt.

```
system:  systemContent
user:    worldNotesBlock + charactersBlock + chapterBlock + taskBlock
```

This bifurcation drove story-editor-9ph: the chat retry path strips the synthesised user message (because the trailing user is already in history), which incidentally strips chapter context for `ask` but not for `scene`.

## Other consequences of the split

- Token-budget calculation in `buildPrompt` has to special-case scene (`fixedTokens` includes `sceneTemplate` instead of `taskBlock`).
- The `ask` history-mapping in `chat.routes.ts:455-466` needs a special branch for prior turns with attachments (re-rendering via `renderAskUserContent`) because the bare-text version in history doesn't match the synthesised version that originally went to Venice. Scene history doesn't need this branch.
- Future actions (e.g. retry-with-instruction, edit-then-regenerate, multi-turn scene continuation) will face the same fork: which action-flavor do they belong to, and where does context go?
- Test surface is doubled: every prompt-shape test has to think about both layouts.

## Proposed direction

Move toward a single canonical message-array layout for all actions, with chapter / characters / world-notes in the **system message** (matches `scene`'s shape, since scene has been the reference for "the model has full context"). The user message becomes the action-specific instruction only.

Concretely:

1. Refactor `buildPrompt` so every action assembles the system message from the same building blocks (`systemContent`, `worldNotesBlock`, `charactersBlock`, `chapterBlock`, `taskBlock` — where `taskBlock` is the action-specific instruction, currently it's the user content).
2. The user message becomes the user's literal input (chat: their question; scene: their direction; inline AI: the selection text + freeform if any).
3. Update token budget to compute `fixedTokens` once across all actions (no scene-vs-other branch).
4. Update `chat.routes.ts` history-mapping to drop the `ask` attachment-rewrap branch (no longer needed — the synthesised content matches the bare history entry by construction).
5. Update `chat.routes.ts:476-478` to use a single `[systemMsg, ...history, userMsg]` form for both retry and non-retry; on retry, exclude the trailing user from history because it's now equivalent to userMsg.

## Wider scope: prompt-building convergence across the app

Beyond `chat.routes.ts` + `prompt.service.ts`, audit:

- `backend/src/routes/ai.routes.ts` (inline AI completion) — uses the same `buildPrompt` but with action `continue`/`rephrase`/etc. Does its message-array assembly differ from chat? If so, normalize.
- Any other prompt-building entry points (search for `buildPrompt(` and `messages: [...` in backend/src). Goal: one renderer, one shape, one set of tests.
- Frontend has no prompt builder (Venice calls are server-side only) — but the frontend-side row/render layer should keep matching the server's notion of "user said X" vs "the system framed X." Right now, what the user typed and what reaches Venice as a `user` message can be very different (e.g. for `ask`, "Can you continue?" becomes a 2KB message with chapter content prepended). Should the user-facing transcript ever expose what was actually sent? Out of scope for this issue, but flag.

## Why not stop-gap path (a) on 9ph

Path (a) on 9ph — filtering `lastUserMsg.id` out of `priorMessagesForHistory` on retry, then unifying the messages array — fixes the immediate symptom but bakes the asymmetry deeper into the route logic. It's another special-case for `ask`'s structural quirk. Don't ship it; fix the root cause.

## Acceptance

- One canonical message-array shape across all `buildPrompt` actions.
- Chat 'ask' retry preserves chapter context (closes 9ph as a side-effect).
- All existing prompt tests pass; new tests added for the unified shape.
- L-series live test (or staging smoke) confirms Venice produces equivalent outputs on the new shape (i.e. the move from user-message-context to system-message-context for `ask` doesn't measurably degrade quality — sample some Chat replies before/after).
- No `if (action === 'scene')` or `if (action === 'ask')` branches remain in `buildPrompt` outside the `taskBlock` itself.
- `chat.routes.ts:455-466` ask-attachment-rewrap branch can be removed.
- Doc the layout decision in `docs/agent-rules/backend.md` (AI section) so future actions inherit it.

## Cross-links

- Closes (or supersedes): story-editor-9ph (Chat retry context drop — same bug, deeper fix).
- Touches: `backend/src/services/prompt.service.ts`, `backend/src/routes/chat.routes.ts`, `backend/src/routes/ai.routes.ts`, `backend/tests/services/prompt.service.test.ts` (or wherever prompt tests live), `backend/tests/routes/chat.test.ts`.
- Spec: needs a brainstorm + plan before implementation. The shape change has L-series implications.
notes:
plan: docs/superpowers/plans/2026-05-10-k1r-prompt-building-unification.md
trivial: design decision required — see description for direction. Brainstorm + plan first; the L-series live test should confirm no Venice output regression on the shape change. verify: cd backend && npm test && (after staging smoke) npm run test:live

title: [Chat/Scene] Tab session default selection picks oldest session, should pick most-recent
description:
When entering a Chat or Scene tab (or switching between them) for a chapter that already has prior sessions, the tab defaults to the first/oldest session in the list instead of the most recently-touched one.

Locations:
- frontend/src/components/ChatTab.tsx — the useEffect that runs setActiveChatId(sessions[0].id) when activeChatId === null && sessions.length > 0.
- frontend/src/components/SceneTab.tsx — same pattern.

Fix paths:
  (a) Sort the sessions query result by updatedAt desc on the server side (in useChatsQuery / its backend endpoint), so sessions[0] is the right pick by construction.
  (b) Sort client-side in the default-select effect: pick sessions reduce-max by updatedAt instead of [0].
  (c) Persist last-active session per (chapterId, kind) in user settings or local storage and restore on tab open.

(a) is cleanest if no other consumer relies on a stable order. (c) is what most chat UIs do but adds state. (b) is the smallest fix.

Session-picker dropdown shows the order independently — verify whether its current order also needs adjusting for consistency.

Reproduction: open a chapter with at least 2 chat sessions, click the most recent one, then leave the chapter and come back (or switch to Scene tab and back to Chat) — Chat tab will land on the oldest session instead of the one the user just used.
notes: verify: cd frontend && npm test -- ChatTab.test.tsx SceneTab.test.tsx

title: [Chat] Per-message Regenerate button is not wired — onRegenerateMessage prop never passed
description:
The Regenerate (Retry) icon button shown next to each assistant message in the Chat transcript does nothing when clicked. Confirmed by reading the wiring:

## Trace

`frontend/src/components/ChatMessages.tsx:40` declares the prop on `ChatMessagesProps`:
```ts
onRegenerateMessage?: (id: string) => void;
```

`ChatMessages.tsx:201-202` defines the click handler:
```ts
const handleRegenerate = (): void => {
  if (onRegenerate) onRegenerate(message.id);
};
```

— this is a guarded call, so when `onRegenerate` is undefined, the click is a silent no-op. The button still renders (lines 227-229: `aria-label="Regenerate"`, `title="Regenerate"`, `onClick={handleRegenerate}`).

`ChatTab.tsx:178-182` mounts `ChatMessages` with only **three** props:
```tsx
<ChatMessages
  chatId={activeChatId}
  sendError={sendChatMessage.error}
  onRetrySend={onRetry}
/>
```

The `onRegenerateMessage` prop is **never passed**, so `onRegenerate` is undefined inside `AssistantMessage`, so the button is dead.

(`onRetrySend` is a different code path — it's wired to the `InlineErrorBanner` for after-an-error retries, not per-message regenerate.)

## What "regenerate" should do

A user clicks Regenerate on the trailing assistant message → the chat replays that turn's user message through Venice with the same model + same context, replacing the assistant message in place (or appending a new candidate, depending on UX choice — see story-editor-a9v which is grappling with the same question for Scene's "candidate" model). The `useSendChatMessageMutation` already accepts a `retry: true` flag (`backend/src/routes/chat.routes.ts` accepts `retry: true` in the body, and `useChat.ts:208-210` types `retry?: boolean`) — the wiring just needs:

1. A handler in `ChatTab` that calls `sendChatMessage.mutate({ chatId, modelId, retry: true })` (no new user content).
2. Pass that handler as `onRegenerateMessage` on the `<ChatMessages>` JSX.

## Open question (might fold into story-editor-a9v)

Per-message regenerate has a UX choice: replace the existing assistant turn, or append a new candidate (Scene-style)? The post-`x29 retry` recent commit `954b1ef [scene-tab] fix: render every assistant message as its own candidate card (retry visible)` settled this for Scene as "append a new candidate." Chat should pick the same model — and that's exactly the question story-editor-a9v is asking. **This bug fix should probably wait for a9v's design pass** so the wired-up Retry behaves consistently with whatever a9v decides; OR, ship a stop-gap "replace trailing assistant message" wiring now and revisit when a9v lands.

## Acceptance

- Clicking Regenerate on an assistant message triggers a Venice round-trip that replays the matching user turn with the same model + context.
- Behaviour aligns with whatever story-editor-a9v decides (replace-in-place vs append-new-candidate). If a9v hasn't landed when this is picked up, file a `block:` link on a9v.
- Tests exercise the click → mutation path. Add a `<ChatTab>` test with a stubbed `apiStream` mocking the SSE response and asserting the second `apiStream` call has `retry: true` in its body.

Touches: `ChatTab.tsx` (handler + prop wiring), `ChatMessages.tsx` (no behavioural change unless a9v decides to add a candidate-list visual), `useChat.ts` (no API change — the `retry` flag is already supported), tests.

trivial: wiring fix; the mutation already supports retry: true. May want to wait for story-editor-a9v's design decision so behaviour matches the candidate-card model. verify: cd frontend && npm run typecheck && npm test && npm run lint:design

title: Retire legacy /task-verify, /bd-close, /bd-link-plan slash skills
description:
The bd-tooling migration (PR #70) and the multi-agent rollout (PRs #73-77) left two parallel close paths: the original /task-verify + /bd-close pair (documented as canonical step 8 in CLAUDE.md's Task Completion Protocol) and the newer /bd-execute + /bd-close-reviewed pair (the multi-agent flow's documented close).

The canonical path documented in CLAUDE.md still points at the older pair, so the multi-agent flow doesn't auto-trigger on session start. Audit (this conversation) confirmed:
- /task-verify: zero invocations in any bd issue notes; CLAUDE.md itself calls it 'optional sanity-check; /bd-close runs the same gate internally'.
- /bd-link-plan: zero invocations in any bd issue notes; one-line wrapper around scripts/bd-link-plan.sh.
- /bd-close: documented as canonical step 8, but functionally a subset of /bd-close-reviewed (no typecheck, no surface-reviewer fan-out).

Net: keep /bd-execute + /bd-close-reviewed; retire the trio. Update CLAUDE.md Task Completion Protocol to make /bd-execute the default flow, drop the three skills' directories, update README + agent-workflow.md cheatsheet to match.

Side benefit: recovers ~700 tokens of skill-listing budget that was tipping /doctor into dropping superpowers' test-driven-development, requesting-code-review, and writing-skills descriptions.
notes:
plan: trivial — doc rewrite + delete three skill dirs (no behaviour change beyond removing now-redundant slash commands); rationale captured inline in this issue's description.

title: [Chat/Scene] @-mention character autocomplete in sidebar; reuse main-editor affordance
description:
The main TipTap editor has an @-mention character picker (the popup that lets you reference characters from the story's character list). The sidebar Chat and Scene composers don't have this — adding it would be a nice quality-of-life win for prompt composition.

Doesn't need the full character-sheet hover popup that the main editor uses — just an autocomplete dropdown that lets the user type @ + name and insert the character's name as a token. Tokens can render as plain text or with a lightweight pill style; the underlying value sent to Venice is just the name.

Pair this with story-editor-bcp (charRef @-mention: token-wise backspace within mention) so the same backspace-deletes-the-whole-token affordance works in the sidebar composers too. story-editor-bcp originally targeted the main editor; the implementation should be shared so this feature gets it for free.

Touches: sidebar ChatComposer / SceneComposer (currently plain textarea — would become TipTap or a lightweight rich-input), and the existing character-mention extension (factor it for reuse if needed).
notes: Plan needed. Cross-reference story-editor-bcp for the backspace token-deletion behavior. Worth deciding upfront whether to upgrade the sidebar composers to TipTap (heavier, but shares the existing extension) vs writing a lighter custom autocomplete (simpler, but duplicates UX logic).

title: [Chat] Remove dead Ask/Rewrite/Describe action buttons; unify send button as Ask + Stop
description:
Two changes to the chat composer area:

1. Remove the 'Ask' / 'Rewrite' / 'Describe' buttons under the message box. Only Ask is wired up; chat is going to be 'ask' going forward, so the row is dead UI. Sweep for dead handlers and tests.

2. Rename the Send button next to the message box to 'Ask' for UX consistency with Scene's 'Generate'. Make it act as a Stop button while streaming, mirroring SceneComposer's idle/streaming behavior. The infrastructure for stop already exists in the chat streaming path (AbortController pattern); just expose it.

Touches: ChatPanel composer area, useChat / useSendChatMessageMutation, any tests that assert on the dead button labels.

Acceptance:
- The dead 3-button row is gone.
- Send button is labelled 'Ask' (or whatever phrasing matches Scene's 'Generate' grammar).
- While streaming, the button shows Stop and clicking aborts the in-flight request.
- No regression in existing chat-send tests (they may need updating for the new label).

title: Settings → Models: Reset to defaults is a server-side no-op
description:
Reset to defaults visibly resets the sliders for one frame, then the values revert. The backend silently keeps the old override values.

Root cause (in backend/src/routes/user-settings.routes.ts via backend/src/lib/deep-merge.ts):

The reset handler in frontend/src/components/SettingsModelsTab.tsx sends:
  PATCH /api/users/me/settings { chat: { overrides: { ...prev, [activeModelId]: {} } } }

The backend persists `deepMerge(stored, patch)`. deepMerge recurses into chat.overrides[modelId]: target is `{ temperature: ..., topP: ... }` (plain object), source is `{}` (plain object), so it recurses again — and a deepMerge with an empty source returns the target unchanged. So the empty entry is a no-op on the server; old override values stay persisted.

Symptom timeline matches:
1. Frontend mergeSettings does shallow merge on `chat`, so the optimistic update replaces chat.overrides wholesale → sliders flash to defaults.
2. PATCH response arrives with the unchanged-server-state → cache reverts → sliders snap back.

Fix: in the PATCH handler, treat each entry in chat.overrides as atomic — replace per-modelId rather than deep-merging into the entry. An empty `{}` then correctly clears that model overrides. The rest of the settings tree continues to deep-merge as before.

Test the fix at the route level: send { chat: { overrides: { m1: {} } } } after a prior PATCH that set { m1: { temperature: 1.5 } }, assert response shows overrides.m1 = {}.

Other models in chat.overrides must not be affected by a single-model reset.
notes:
plan: trivial: 1-route fix + 1 backend test. Per-modelId atomic replacement inside chat.overrides; rest of deepMerge unchanged.

title: [AI] Remove dead 'freeform' action from /api/ai/complete + prompt.service
description:
The `freeform` AI completion action is dead UI surface — accepted by `/api/ai/complete` and handled by `prompt.service.ts`, but no frontend code dispatches it.

## Evidence

- `backend/src/routes/ai.routes.ts:32` — accepted in the action enum.
- `backend/src/routes/ai.routes.ts:43-50` — refine guard requires `freeformInstruction`.
- `backend/src/services/prompt.service.ts:17` — listed in `PromptAction`.
- `backend/src/services/prompt.service.ts:154-157` — `buildTaskBlock` arm.
- `frontend/src/hooks/useAICompletion.ts:41` — typed in `RunArgs['action']` union.
- **No frontend call site** — `SelectionBubble` (`'rewrite' | 'describe' | 'expand' | 'ask'`) and `ContinueWriting` (`'continue'`) cover the only inline AI surfaces. Searched: `grep -rEn "action: ['\"]freeform" frontend/src` returns only the type union itself, no dispatches.
- Discovered during k1r (story-editor-k1r) review while resolving the unified prompt-builder template lookup. Documented as out-of-scope for k1r in `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-10-k1r-prompt-building-unification-design.md`.

## Scope

- Remove `'freeform'` from `PromptAction` (`backend/src/services/prompt.service.ts`).
- Remove the `freeform` arm from `buildUserPayload` (post-k1r) / `buildTaskBlock` (pre-k1r).
- Remove `'freeform'` from the `/api/ai/complete` zod enum + drop the `freeformInstruction`-required refine.
- Remove `'freeform'` from `RunArgs['action']` in `frontend/src/hooks/useAICompletion.ts`.
- Drop tests that exercise the freeform action specifically (search `backend/tests` and `frontend/tests` for `freeform` and audit each hit — some may be testing other parts of the prompt builder and the test data just happens to use `'freeform'`).
- Update any backend `freeformInstruction is required when action is "freeform"` references in tests / fixtures.

## Backwards-compatibility note

This is a deliberate API break — anyone hitting `/api/ai/complete` directly with `action: 'freeform'` would get a 400. Acceptable per CLAUDE.md "no data-migration branches" + the fact that the surface has no Inkwell consumers. If a power-user is using it, they'll see the change in the next release notes.

## Acceptance

- `grep -rEn "freeform" backend/src frontend/src` returns no hits other than this issue's history.
- `cd backend && npm test` and `cd frontend && npm test` pass after removals.
- The `/api/ai/complete` route rejects `action: 'freeform'` with the standard zod 400.

## Sequencing

After story-editor-k1r merges. (k1r touches the same `buildPrompt` switch arm; landing this first would create avoidable conflict.)

title: [Chat/Scene] Retry visual: thinking + streaming reply briefly appear below old assistant before flash-replace
description:
On clicking Regenerate on an assistant message:

1. ChatTab/SceneTab call sendChatMessage.mutateAsync({chatId, modelId, retry: true}).
2. onMutate runs useChatDraftStore.start({chatId, ...}) which seeds a fresh draft slot.
3. TranscriptView's buildRows() appends the draft-assistant row AFTER the persisted messages (which still include the old assistant being retried).
4. Result: thinking dots render in a new row BELOW the old assistant. As tokens stream, the new content fills the row below the old.
5. Backend's deleteAllAfter runs server-side; on success, the cache invalidates and refetches; the old assistant vanishes and the new persists in its place — visible as a flash-replace.

Technically correct (the linear-replace semantics from PR #90 land correctly) but visually messy: there's a window where two assistants exist for the same user prompt — the soon-to-be-deleted old one and the streaming-in-place new one.

Observed on both Chat and Scene tabs after PR #90.

Design question (this needs the redesign hat, not a quick fix):
  (a) Optimistically remove the trailing assistant from the cache when retry starts (matching what the backend will do), so the draft-assistant appears in the slot the old one occupied. Risk: if the mutation fails after persistence has been deleted, we'd need to restore.
  (b) Render the draft-assistant inline at the position of the assistant being retried (turns the merge logic from append-after-persisted to splice-at-trailing-assistant when draft is a retry vs a fresh send).
  (c) Hide the old trailing assistant from the rendered row stream while the retry mutation is in-flight (CSS-level suppression in TranscriptView's render-prop or buildRows).
  (d) Some combination — e.g. (c) for the visual + (a) for the cache to keep the merge logic simple.

Need to decide the right model before coding. Filing now per user direction; revisit when AI surfaces stabilises (likely after story-editor-a9v's per-message UX furniture pass settles, though that closed in PR #90).

Related:
- TranscriptView.tsx buildRows() — current append-only merge.
- useChat.ts mutationFn — onMutate calls start(); onSuccess calls clear() + invalidate.
- chat.routes.ts retry branch — runs deleteAllAfter before generating.

Not a regression: the old SceneCandidateCard model showed BOTH old and new as candidate cards (with isLatest/superseded markers), which was visually clearer but architecturally diverged. The new model is correct but ugly during the transition.
notes: trivial: design decision required — see description for fix-path options.\nverify: TBD after design

title: [Frontend] AI-error reporting policy: bubble-only vs global toast — pick one, apply uniformly
description:
Today AI errors are reported inconsistently across the three AI surfaces:

- **Chat** (`useSendChatMessageMutation`): bubble-scoped via the draft store's `error` field; renders inline error banner attached to the bubble. NOT pushed to global `useErrorStore`.
- **Scene** (today; converges with Chat after the AI-surfaces unification PR): bubble-scoped via scene store's `failAssistant`; renders `InlineErrorBanner` above the transcript.
- **Inline AI** (`useAICompletion`): bubble-shown AND globally published to `useErrorStore`. The error appears both in the inline-AI card AND in the global error toast/list.

Net result for the user: inline-AI failures feel noisier than chat failures, with no intentional product reason behind it. The 'should AI errors hit the global toast?' question has one right answer per the app's overall error model — we're just not picking it.

## Brainstorm-first

This is a design question, not just a mechanical refactor. Possible models:

1. **All AI errors bubble-only.** Each surface owns its own error display; the global toast is reserved for system-level errors (auth expired, network out, etc.). Quietest UX. Inline AI loses global visibility — the user only sees the error if the inline-AI card is visible. Probably the right call if AI errors are typically recoverable in-context (re-run, edit prompt).

2. **All AI errors hit the global toast (and show in the bubble).** Loudest UX. The user can't miss an AI failure even if they've moved on to another part of the app. Probably the right call if AI errors often indicate a setup problem (key invalid, account locked) that's not obvious from the surface alone.

3. **Hybrid: severity-based.** Recoverable errors (rate limit, transient stream error) stay bubble-scoped; setup errors (no key, key invalid, account suspended, model unavailable) hit global. Most ergonomic but requires categorising every error code.

## Out of scope

- The error banner visual design itself.
- Adding new surfaces beyond the three current AI surfaces.
notes: trivial: design decision required before plan/execute — see description for evaluation directions. Brainstorm-first. verify: TBD after brainstorm.

title: [Frontend] Unify Venice rate-limit indicator across Chat + inline AI
description:
Venice returns `x-venice-remaining-requests` and `x-venice-remaining-tokens` on every API response. Today only `useAICompletion` (lines 179-186) harvests them and stores in its component-local `usage` field. The chat path (`useSendChatMessageMutation`) doesn't bother — same headers come back on `POST /chats/:chatId/messages`, ignored.

This is divergence for no reason. Both surfaces should feed one store and one user-visible indicator should reflect "remaining Venice budget" regardless of which AI surface most recently spent some.

## Direction

After the AI-surfaces unification PR ships:
- `runStreamingAI` already exposes `onResponseHeaders?: (res: Response) => void` in its signature (added in that PR — lets future harvesting drop into either consumer without rewiring the wire-protocol layer).
- Add a small `useApiUsageStore` Zustand slice with `{ remainingRequests, remainingTokens }` and a `record(headers)` method that preserves prior snapshot when headers absent (current useAICompletion behavior).
- Both `useSendChatMessageMutation` and `useAICompletion` wire `onResponseHeaders → useApiUsageStore.getState().record(...)`.
- The user-visible indicator (currently surfaced only via `useAICompletion.usage`) reads from the shared store. Single source of truth.
- `useAICompletion`'s component-local `usage` field can be removed; consumers switch to the shared store.

## Acceptance

- One shared store fed by both AI surfaces.
- One user-visible indicator reading from it.
- Existing inline-AI usage display unchanged from the user's perspective; chat sends now also update it.

title: [Chat/Scene] Extract shared transcript-container component (Chat autoscroll falls out for free)
description:
**Original framing was too narrow.** The starting observation is real: Chat doesn't auto-scroll on new messages and Scene does. But the right fix isn't "port Scene's snippet to Chat" — it's "the transcript-container layer (scroll + pin-to-bottom + session-reset) should live in one component that both tabs consume." Then autoscroll lands once, and any future improvement (load-more older messages, virtualisation when sessions get long, scroll-to-citation, scroll-to-mention) also lands once.

## What "transcript container" means

The data flow is identical between Chat and Scene:
- Same backend endpoint (`POST /api/chats/:chatId/messages` with `kind='ask'` or `kind='scene'`).
- Same query hook (`useChatMessagesQuery(chatId)`).
- Same persistence shape (`ChatMessage[]`).
- Same streaming behaviour (eventually unified by `story-editor-a0s`).

The only thing that differs is **how each row is rendered** — Chat shows flat user/assistant messages; Scene shows assistant turns as candidate cards (with isLatest / superseded semantics). That row-rendering difference is `story-editor-a9v`'s territory; this issue is one layer up.

## Current state (snapshots)

- **Scene container** lives inline in `frontend/src/components/SceneTab.tsx:277-292` — a `transcriptRef` on the scroll div, an effect that runs on each new message computing `distanceFromBottom = el.scrollHeight - el.scrollTop - el.clientHeight`, with a session-change reset.
- **Chat container** is just `<div className="flex-1 min-h-0 overflow-y-auto">` in `frontend/src/components/ChatTab.tsx:177-183`. No ref, no autoscroll, no session reset.

Recent Scene commits to learn from:
- `b87758c [scene-tab] autoscroll transcript on new messages, respecting user scroll-up`
- `84b2dd1 [scene-tab] fix: reset autoscroll on session switch`

## Direction

Extract the container layer as a small component (working name `TranscriptView`):

```tsx
<TranscriptView chatId={…}>
  {/* row renderer — Chat passes ChatMessages internals; Scene passes SceneCandidateCard list */}
</TranscriptView>
```

Owns: the scroll container element, the ref, the pin-to-bottom effect, the session-change reset.

Doesn't own: the row markup (a9v's territory), the data fetch (`useChatMessagesQuery` stays in the consumer or moves into the container — design decision).

## Open design questions

1. **Render-prop vs children?** Children are simpler; render-prop gives the container access to the data array (useful for "show new-messages-below indicator when user scrolled up"). The latter pairs well with future improvements.
2. **Where does the data fetch live?** Inside `TranscriptView` (one less consumer concern, but couples the container to the chat-message hook) or outside (more flexible, slightly more boilerplate at each call site)?
3. **What about the optimistic draft?** Chat has `useChatDraftStore` for the streaming bubble; Scene has its own draft mechanism. The container needs to "see" both as one logical row stream — this is again where `story-editor-a0s` (unified streaming hook) intersects. May want to wait for a0s to land before this so the draft-store integration is one unified primitive.

## Acceptance

- One `TranscriptView` (or equivalent) is the only place autoscroll/session-reset logic lives.
- ChatTab.tsx and SceneTab.tsx both consume it; no duplicate scroll-container code.
- Chat gets autoscroll as a side effect (the original y5v ask).
- Tests cover the autoscroll + session-reset behaviour at the container level (consumer-side tests don't have to re-assert it).
- Storybook coverage of the container with multiple row-renderer fixtures.

## Cross-links

- `story-editor-a9v` — per-message furniture alignment. Different layer (row content vs container). Should ship together but as separate PRs OR one stacked PR; sequence so a9v's per-row component renders inside this container, not the other way around.
- `story-editor-a0s` — streaming consolidation (unified `useStreamingAI` primitive). Pairs with this — together they unify rendering + data layers. Sequencing TBD; possibly a0s first so the draft-store integration in this container is built against the final shape.

## Sequencing recommendation

Brainstorm the trio (a0s + y5v + a9v) together once before picking up any of them, since they touch the same surface and the shape choices interact. The bd dependencies should reflect: y5v can be picked up independently as a stop-gap "just port the snippet" — file as a derivative issue if going that route — but the architecturally correct path is the trio.

title: [Chat/Scene] Align per-message UX 'furniture' across the two transcripts
description:
The two AI-conversation surfaces — Chat (`ChatMessages.tsx`) and Scene (`SceneCandidateCard.tsx`) — show different per-message affordances. Both render the same kind of artifact (an assistant turn from Venice on a chapter), but the meta-row underneath each message diverges and the two are slowly drifting further apart.

## Observed divergence (snapshot of current code)

**Chat per-message has:**
- Token count + latency stat (`tokens` from `usage.total_tokens` + `latencyMs`)
- Copy button
- Regenerate (Retry) button — on every assistant message
- Attached-context preview (chapter · characters · tokens-in-context)
- No model name displayed
- No "this one is current vs superseded" semantics

**Scene per-message (per candidate card) has:**
- Model name displayed
- "Insert at end" affordance (folds the candidate into the chapter body)
- Retry button — **only on the latest candidate**
- Copy button
- "superseded" marker on non-latest candidates
- No token count, no latency display
- No attached-context preview

## Why it matters

Whatever lands in `story-editor-e95` (token-usage visibility) will only fix Chat unless we also align Scene. More broadly, the bw2 PR aligned the **composer** between the two tabs — the per-message furniture is the natural next pass. Right now a user moving from Chat to Scene has to learn two different vocabularies for the same kind of action ("retry the last reply"; "insert this content"), and we maintain two parallel sets of rendering code.

## What "aligned" means — open design question

Not a strict copy-paste:
- Scene's "candidate" semantics (multiple parallel attempts, latest-wins, insert-at-end as a first-class action) are **load-bearing for Scene's UX** and shouldn't disappear.
- Chat's flat-history semantics (linear conversation, regenerate replaces the trailing turn) are load-bearing for Chat.

So the question is: what's shared, what's tab-specific?

**Likely shared:**
- Model badge (always show which model produced each turn)
- Token + latency stat (with the breakdown from e95 once that lands)
- Copy button
- Citations row (when present — Chat already has this; Scene's `kind='scene'` chats also receive citations from the same backend path)
- Error state styling (inline error banner per turn vs global)

**Likely tab-specific:**
- Scene: "Insert at end" + "superseded" marker + retry-only-on-latest
- Chat: Regenerate-on-every-turn + attached-context preview

**Not yet decided:** whether the shared pieces become a single component with slots for tab-specific affordances, or two components that consume a shared `MessageStats` / `MessageActions` primitive set. The frontend rules in `docs/agent-rules/frontend.md` favour token-only Tailwind + composition over big monolithic components — leaning towards the primitives-with-slots approach, but a brainstorm should confirm.

## Acceptance (placeholder — needs design pass)

- The shared affordances render identically across Chat and Scene (same icons, same labels, same hover states, same a11y).
- The tab-specific affordances stay where they belong (no Chat showing "Insert at end"; no Scene showing the attached-context preview).
- Storybook coverage for the shared primitive(s).
- Whatever lands in story-editor-e95 (token-usage visibility) drops into Scene as a side effect of the alignment.

Touches: `ChatMessages.tsx`, `SceneCandidateCard.tsx`, possibly a new `frontend/src/components/MessageStats.tsx` (or similar) primitive, related Storybook stories, the test suites for both components.

Related: `story-editor-e95` (token-usage visibility — should ship after this so the design lands once, not twice). `story-editor-a0s` (P1 streaming consolidation — orthogonal, but the rendering layer this issue touches will be touched again when streaming consolidates, so sequencing matters).
design:
**Brainstorm-first.** The implementation is small but the design choice (slot-based shared primitive vs two-components-with-helpers) is load-bearing for how `story-editor-e95` and future per-message features land. Recommend `superpowers:brainstorming` covering:

1. **Inventory pass.** Enumerate every meta-row affordance on each side, decide shared vs tab-specific. Use the snapshot above as a starting point.
2. **Component shape.** One of:
   - **Single shared component with slots** — `<MessageMeta tokens={…} latency={…} model={…} actions={<>{...}</>} />` consumed by both. Cleanest if the shared set is large.
   - **Shared primitives, tab-specific compositions** — `<TokenBadge />`, `<ModelBadge />`, `<CopyAction />` exported from a `frontend/src/components/messageMeta/` directory; `ChatMessages` and `SceneCandidateCard` compose them. More flexible if affordances vary a lot.
   - **No shared layer; just align styling** — keep the two components separate, copy the styles. Lowest churn, doesn't reduce drift over time. Probably the wrong call.
3. **Sequencing with e95.** Should this issue land first (so e95's token UI drops into both surfaces for free), or e95 first (so the design has a concrete usage)? Argument for this-first: avoids re-doing the e95 design twice.
4. **Storybook expectations.** If we build a shared primitive, it needs its own stories file (the bw2 PR established the convention).
5. **Test surface.** Both `ChatMessages.test.tsx` and a Scene-side test exist. The shared primitive (if we go that way) needs its own tests; tab-specific tests stay in the consumer.

## Possible UX deviations to keep in mind

- Scene's "superseded" marker is information-dense and useful — should Chat get an analogous "this turn was regenerated, viewing #2 of 3" affordance? That's a scope question for the brainstorm.
- Chat's attached-context preview (chapter · characters · tokens) might be useful in Scene too — Scene also attaches the chapter context, just doesn't display it.
- Error states diverge today (Chat has a per-turn error banner; Scene shows the error in the candidate card itself). The shared design should pick one model.

## Out of scope

- Backend changes. The data model already exposes everything both surfaces need (`tokens`, `latencyMs`, `model`, `citationsJson`).
- Composer changes. bw2 already aligned the composers.
- Streaming behaviour. That's `story-editor-a0s`.

title: [Chat] Token-usage visibility + bounded history (truncation) — needs UX design
description:
Two related gaps in how chat token usage is surfaced and managed:

## Gap A: History never truncates → eventual 413 / model-rejection on long chats

`backend/src/routes/chat.routes.ts:433-458` assembles the messages array as: system + ALL prior turns from the chat (via `findManyForChat(chatId)` in `backend/src/repos/message.repo.ts:78-86`) + the new user turn. The token-budget step in `backend/src/services/prompt.service.ts:176-227` only trims the **chapter body** — never drops oldest turns from the history. A long-running chat session will eventually exceed the model's context window, at which point Venice rejects the request. There's no graceful fallback: no oldest-turn pruning, no rolling-summary collapse, no user-visible warning.

## Gap B: Per-message token badge is opaque — masks reasoning-model variance

`frontend/src/components/ChatMessages.tsx:235` renders `${message.tokens} tok` from `usage.total_tokens` captured at `backend/src/routes/chat.routes.ts:678-680`. That single number is `prompt_tokens + completion_tokens` lumped together. For reasoning models (Qwen QwQ, DeepSeek R1, etc.) where `strip_thinking_response: true` strips chain-of-thought from the visible content but Venice still charges for those tokens, two fresh sessions asking the same question can produce wildly different totals (observed: 2448 vs 2890 on identical "suggest a chapter title" prompts). Users can't tell why because the breakdown isn't surfaced.

## Why one issue, not two

Both gaps are aspects of the same UX problem: chat token usage is invisible until it bites. A user who can see "2400 prompt + 50 visible + 440 reasoning" understands why a fresh session costs 2900 tokens; a user who can also see "session at 18k / 32k context window" knows when they're about to hit the limit and can compact / start fresh. Designing them together avoids two passes at the same composer-footer real estate.

## Acceptance (placeholder — needs design pass)

- Per-message badge shows enough breakdown to diagnose reasoning-model variance.
- Session-level usage indicator shows current context-window utilisation.
- Long chats degrade gracefully (oldest-turn pruning, rolling summary, hard-cap warning, or some combination — design decision).
- No backend change to what we send to Venice without explicit design sign-off (history pruning IS a behavioural change).

Touches: ChatMessages.tsx (badge), ChatComposer.tsx or a new sibling (session-level indicator), useChatMessagesQuery / message persistence (capture prompt/completion split separately), prompt.service.ts (history truncation), the same surface for Scene chats (kind='scene').
design:
**This issue needs a brainstorm + design pass before plan/execute.** The implementation is mostly straightforward once UX is settled; the hard part is deciding what to surface and how. Below are starting points for evaluation, NOT a settled approach.

## Per-message token breakdown (Gap B) — possible UI directions

1. **Tooltip on the existing badge.** Keep `2890 tok` as the visible default; on hover, expand to `2400 in · 50 out · 440 reasoning` (or whatever Venice's `usage` object exposes). Lowest visual cost; discoverable only by users who hover.
2. **Inline split.** Replace `2890 tok` with `2400→490` (in→out) or `2400 + 490 tok`. Doesn't expose reasoning separately unless we add a third number. Risks getting noisy.
3. **Settings toggle "show detailed usage".** Default to compact; power users opt in. Avoids visual clutter for casual users.
4. **Click-to-expand a row of usage dots / pill.** Below the timestamp; tapping reveals breakdown. Mobile-friendly.

Open question: does Venice's SSE `usage` chunk actually expose reasoning tokens separately, or only `prompt_tokens` and `completion_tokens`? If only the two-way split, "reasoning" disappears as a dimension and (1) collapses to a tooltip showing in/out only — still useful, since identical-prompt sessions should then have near-identical numbers and any drift is observable. Verify against Venice docs via Context7 MCP at design time.

## Session-level context-window indicator (Gap A) — possible UI directions

1. **Fill bar in the composer footer.** Thin progress bar showing `(running token total) / (model context length)`. Colour shifts to amber at ~75%, danger at ~90%. Visible at all times; cost is one row of footer.
2. **Numerical readout next to "⌘↵ to send".** `12.4k / 32k` — terse, ignorable until it matters. No visual fill.
3. **Lazy warning.** No baseline indicator; show a banner only when nearing the limit (e.g. ≥85%). Lowest noise; users get caught off guard if the warning misses by a turn.
4. **Per-message running counter.** Each assistant message annotates the *cumulative* session cost ("2400 this turn / 12k cumulative"). Less actionable than a footer indicator; more historically informative.

Open question: where does the running total live? Computed client-side from the last assistant message's `tokens` value (works because it includes the full prompt at that point — though it doesn't include the next turn's user message), or backend-computed and returned in a header on each send? Client-side is cheaper but trails reality by one turn.

## History-pruning behaviour (Gap A) — possible algorithms

1. **Hard FIFO drop.** When prompt + new turn > budget, drop oldest turns until it fits. Simple; loses early context users may still care about.
2. **Rolling summary.** When approaching budget, summarise the oldest N turns into a single system-injected `<summary>...</summary>` block, then drop the originals from the wire. Most chat apps do this. Adds a Venice round-trip cost for the summarisation.
3. **User-triggered "compact".** Show a "Compact older messages" button when nearing limit; user opts in. Most transparent; requires user action.
4. **Hard cap with explicit cliff.** Refuse to send if it would exceed budget; tell the user "this chat is full, start a new session". Crudest; preserves perfect fidelity for what does get sent.

The Scene tab (kind='scene') has the same problem and should adopt the same solution — think of these as orthogonal concerns of the same chat-message endpoint, not Chat-only.

## Brainstorm-first

Recommend: superpowers:brainstorming with the user covering (a) which UX direction for each gap, (b) whether to ship the two gaps as one PR or stack them, (c) whether the Scene tab gets it for free or needs separate visual treatment. Then writing-plans, then /bd-execute.

The token-breakdown UI is small and ship-able alone if the truncation design needs longer to settle; the truncation work is the riskier behavioural change and benefits from being its own PR.
notes: trivial: design decision required before plan/execute — see --design for evaluation directions. verify: TBD after brainstorm.

title: charRef @-mention: token-wise backspace within mention
description:
When the user backspaces inside a charRef-marked run inserted via @-mention, deletion should be token-wise (Messenger-style) rather than character-wise: a single Backspace removes the entire trailing whitespace-separated token of the mark in one operation.

Example: inserting 'Elanor Vance' via @ produces a single charRef-marked run 'Elanor Vance'. Backspace at the end of that run deletes 'Vance' (and the preceding space within the mark) in one step, leaving 'Elanor' still marked. A subsequent Backspace then deletes 'Elanor' in one step.

Scope: only applies to text covered by the charRef mark (inserted via the @-suggestion in frontend/src/lib/charRefSuggestion.ts). Plain typed text outside the mark keeps default per-character backspace behavior. Forward-Delete (the Delete key) is out of scope unless trivially symmetric.

Implementation sketch: a ProseMirror keymap on Backspace that detects when the cursor sits at the end of (or inside) a charRef-marked range with no selection, finds the previous whitespace boundary inside the mark, and replaces that span with an empty insertion in one tx. If the deletion would empty the mark, drop the mark entirely so the result is a clean caret.

Also handle: cursor inside the middle of a charRef run — backspace deletes from caret back to the previous in-mark whitespace boundary (or to the mark start). Selection-active backspace stays default (it already removes the selection).
notes:
plan: trivial: scoped UX polish — single keymap handler in frontend/src/lib/charRefSuggestion.ts (or a sibling extension). No schema, route, or repo changes.

title: DEFAULT_SETTINGS backend/frontend parity test
description:
The settings JSON shape is duplicated between backend and frontend with nothing enforcing parity:

- backend/src/routes/user-settings.routes.ts:132 — DEFAULT_SETTINGS used as merge floor on GET/PATCH
- frontend/src/hooks/useUserSettings.ts:134 — DEFAULT_SETTINGS used as sync fallback, optimistic-update base, and Storybook fixture root

X28 demonstrated the dual-edit obligation: the chat shape changed and both copies got hand-updated. A parity test would have made the obligation explicit and caught any drift at CI time.

Pattern to follow: backend/tests/lib/text-gen-defaults.test.ts (the GLOBAL_TEXT_GEN_DEFAULTS parity test) already does this for the smaller constant. Extend the same idea to DEFAULT_SETTINGS:

1. Export DEFAULT_SETTINGS from backend/src/routes/user-settings.routes.ts (currently a local const).
2. Add a test (location TBD — probably backend/tests/routes/user-settings.parity.test.ts) that reads frontend/src/hooks/useUserSettings.ts via node:fs, extracts the DEFAULT_SETTINGS literal, parses it (or regex-checks key fields), and asserts deep-equal against the backend constant.
3. Optionally, a tsd-style type-shape parity check via expectTypeOf or a generated JSON Schema — out of scope for v1; values-only is enough to catch the X28-style drift.

Out of scope: actually moving DEFAULT_SETTINGS to a single source (API endpoint or shared package). Both have downsides for this constant — see PR #83 conversation for the analysis. Parity test is the cheaper fix.
notes:
trivial: small test file, follows the existing GLOBAL_TEXT_GEN_DEFAULTS parity-test pattern (backend/tests/lib/text-gen-defaults.test.ts:13). One-step "export the constant + write the parity test" — no design decisions.

title: [Frontend] TranscriptView trailing-user content-type assumption
description:
In frontend/src/components/messageRow/TranscriptView.tsx, buildRows() suppresses the draft-user row when the trailing persisted user message's contentJson matches draft.userContent (mid-stream-error path, prevents duplicate-user flicker post-refetch).

The match uses getMessageText(trailingUser.contentJson) === draft.userContent. getMessageText handles strings directly; non-strings get JSON.stringify'd. draft.userContent is always a plain string.

If user messages ever switch to TipTap JSON (or any non-string contentJson), the comparison would never match and the duplicate-user flicker would return on the mid-stream-error path. Today the backend stores user messages as plain strings, so this works.

Fix path: either (a) add a dedicated extractUserText(contentJson) helper that normalises both sides to plain text canonically, OR (b) add a runtime invariant check + test that user contentJson is always string at the type boundary.

Not a current bug — flagged because the suppression rule is non-obvious and the assumption is undocumented.
notes: verify: cd frontend && npm test -- TranscriptView.test.tsx
